title: Checking and adjusting a pressure reducing valve
subtitle: High mains pressure wastes water and strains fittings — how to measure and correct it
excerpt: UK mains water pressure is often 3-6 bar, higher than any household fitting requires. Above 3 bar, toilets refill faster than necessary, shower flow rates increase, and leaks at fittings are more likely. A pressure reducing valve set to 2-2.5 bar cuts all three.
sourceNotes: Water Regulations Advisory Scheme (WRAS) guidance on pressure reducing valves in domestic installations. CIPHE technical notes on mains pressure management. Watts Industries PRV installation and adjustment guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A valve fitted to the main supply pipe that automatically reduces incoming mains pressure to a set lower pressure. Typical domestic setting is 2-3 bar. The PRV is usually located where the supply pipe enters the building, close to the stop tap.

content.text: If the property does not have a PRV and you intend to fit one, the work must comply with Water Regulations (Water Supply (Water Fittings) Regulations 1999). Fitting or adjusting a PRV on the supply pipe is a plumber's task if you are not confident working on the incoming supply.

is typically a brass body with a spring-loaded diaphragm and an adjustment screw under a cap. Most are factory-set to 3 bar. The optimal domestic setting for water efficiency is 2-2.5 bar: high enough for comfortable shower pressure and adequate flow, low enough to avoid wasted flow at taps and cisterns that are nominally rated at 1-3 bar.

Fit a pressure gauge to any accessible tap or washing machine connection using a standard hose-thread adapter. Turn on the tap with the gauge attached and read the static pressure (gauge should stabilise within a few seconds). A reading above 3 bar indicates the PRV is absent, failed, or set too high. A reading below 1.5 bar indicates either low mains pressure or a PRV set too low.
Locate the PRV, normally on the rising main near the stop tap, often in the airing cupboard or under the kitchen sink.
Remove the cap on the top of the PRV body; it usually unscrews by hand or with a spanner.

The adjustment screw or nut is visible inside. Clockwise increases the outlet pressure; anti-clockwise decreases it. A quarter-turn changes the outlet pressure by approximately 0.5-1 bar on most domestic PRVs.
Adjust the screw, then check the downstream pressure using the gauge. Repeat until the reading stabilises at 2-2.5 bar.
Replace the cap. Check the PRV body and any downstream fittings for leaks.

If the property has no PRV, a plumber should fit one. The PRV must be fitted after any branch to an outside tap (the outside tap is allowed to run at mains pressure; the garden hose is the pressure-bearing component and is designed for it). The PRV is fitted before the internal distribution so the reduced pressure serves all internal fittings.

Reducing pressure from 4 bar to 2 bar cuts flow through any given orifice by roughly 30 percent (flow varies with the square root of pressure). A shower running at 12 litres per minute at 4 bar will run at approximately 8.5 litres per minute at 2 bar through the same head, without fitting a flow restrictor. For a household that wants both a PRV and a flow restrictor, fit the PRV first and re-measure before choosing the restrictor size.
